description: Safety requirements by task type for pipe fitting, welding, rigging, and industrial erection work

PPE: FR clothing — long sleeves, no cuffs
Ear plugs when overhead or in confined areas
Respiratory protection if galvanized, stainless, or chrome-moly (hex chrome fumes)
hazards: Arc flash/UV burns (eye, skin)
Molten slag ejection
Hex chrome fumes from stainless/chrome-moly
Zinc fumes from galvanized (metal fume fever)
Burns from hot metal and slag
Fire from sparks/slag (up to 35 ft travel)
Electrical shock (especially wet conditions)
Confined space fume accumulation
permits_required: Confined space permit if in vessel/tank
Radiation work permit if near radiography
JSA_items: Verify fire watch assigned and equipped
Check welding leads for damage — no bare spots
Verify ventilation/exhaust for fume control
Confirm electrode oven operational (7018 must be kept at 250-300F)
Ground clamp within 10 ft of work
Remove combustibles within 35 ft or cover with fire blankets
Check for overhead workers who could be hit by slag
Verify scaffold/platform is rated for welding (no aluminum planks)

fire_watch_requirements.during_welding: Continuous fire watch with extinguisher within reach
fire_watch_requirements.after_welding: 30 minutes minimum, 60 minutes for elevated/combustible areas
fire_watch_requirements.extinguisher_type: ABC dry chemical minimum 20 lb, or as specified by site

PPE: Welding hood (shade 8-10 for TIG, lower amperage)
hazards: Intense UV radiation (even more than SMAW due to clean arc)
Argon gas displacement in confined spaces — ODORLESS, HEAVIER THAN AIR
Thoriated tungsten is mildly radioactive (grinding dust)
High-frequency start can interfere with pacemakers
Burns from hot filler rod and base metal
Ozone generation from UV hitting oxygen
permits_required: Confined space permit if in vessel/tank
JSA_items: Verify argon bottle secured upright and regulator in good condition
Check gas flow rate (15-25 CFH typical)
Verify purge setup for pipe (dam, purge gas flow, O2 monitoring)
Confirm ventilation — argon displaces oxygen in low areas
O2 monitor required in confined/semi-confined spaces with argon
Use ceriated or lanthanated tungsten instead of thoriated when possible
If grinding thoriated tungsten, use local exhaust ventilation

purge_requirements.carbon_steel: Purge preferred but not always required, depends on spec
purge_requirements.stainless_steel: MANDATORY — purge to below 1% O2, many specs require 0.5% or less
purge_requirements.titanium: MANDATORY — purge to below 50 ppm O2, trailing shield required
purge_requirements.purge_gas: Argon (most common), nitrogen acceptable for some carbon steel applications
purge_requirements.monitoring: Use oxygen analyzer at purge exit dam to verify O2 level before striking arc

hazards: VERY HIGH fume generation — worst of common processes
Heavy spatter — burns through clothing faster
Slag entrapment if not cleaned between passes
CO2 gas displacement in confined spaces
Wire feed mechanism pinch points
Electromagnetic fields from high amperage
JSA_items: Mandatory fume extraction/ventilation — higher fume rate than SMAW
Extra fire watch diligence due to heavy spatter
Inspect wire feeder and gun liner
Verify gas type matches WPS (CO2 vs 75/25 Ar/CO2)
Wind screens needed — gas shielded FCAW affected by wind over 5 mph
Self-shielded FCAW acceptable in wind but check WPS

PPE: Respiratory protection if cutting stainless, galvanized, or coated materials
hazards: Flying debris and sparks (grinder discs can shatter)
Kickback from grinder catching on work
Burns from hot metal and sparks
Noise exposure (grinders typically 95-105 dB)
Silica dust if cutting concrete
Oxy-fuel flashback and hose fire
Acetylene is explosive above 15 psi — NEVER exceed
Propane/propylene heavier than air — pools in low spots
permits_required: Hot work permit for any spark-producing work
JSA_items: Inspect grinder disc for cracks, correct RPM rating, correct guard position
Never use cut-off wheel for grinding or grind wheel for cutting
Check oxy-fuel hoses for leaks (soapy water test)
Flashback arrestors installed on both regulators
Acetylene pressure never above 15 psi
Secure gas bottles upright, caps on when moving
Minimum 20 ft between oxygen and fuel gas in storage
No oil or grease on oxygen equipment — spontaneous combustion risk
Blanket or shield combustibles below cutting operations

PPE: Fall protection if on load or at height during rigging
hazards: Struck by — #1 killer in rigging operations
Crushing between load and fixed object
Rigging failure (overload, damaged slings, wrong hardware)
Crane tip-over
Boom contact with power lines (10 ft minimum clearance, 20 ft for >350kV)
Wind effects on load (stop lifts typically at 20-25 mph sustained)
Pinch points during load setting
Tag line entanglement
permits_required: Crane lift permit/plan for all lifts
Critical lift plan for loads >75% capacity, dual crane lifts, lifts over occupied areas, blind lifts
Hot work permit if welding after setting
JSA_items: Verify crane inspection current (annual + daily pre-use)
Check all rigging gear — slings, shackles, spreader bars (color-coded quarterly inspection)
Verify load weight including rigging weight
Check ground conditions for outrigger/crawler stability
Identify overhead obstructions and power lines
Establish swing radius barricade
Confirm signal person designated and qualified
Review hand signals with crew
Tag lines on all loads — minimum 2 for long/wide loads
Never stand under suspended load
Wind speed monitoring — suspend operations per plan
Verify lift radius and capacity from load chart

PPE: Full body harness (inspected before each use)
Anchor point rated for 5,000 lbs per person
hazards: Falls — leading cause of death in construction
Dropped objects injuring workers below
Scaffold collapse
Ladder tip-over
Aerial lift tip-over or ejection
Wind at height
Heat stress — more severe at height with harness
JSA_items: Inspect harness — stitching, D-rings, buckles, labels legible
Inspect lanyard — no cuts, burns, chemical damage
Verify anchor point — rated, independent of work platform
Calculate fall distance — 6ft lanyard + 3.5ft deceleration + 6ft worker height = 15.5ft minimum clearance
SRL preferred where fall clearance is limited
All tools lanyard'd — dropped wrench from 100 ft is lethal
Barricade below for overhead work
Scaffold tag must be green — inspect before each shift
Never climb on piping, steel, or equipment without approved access
Wind policy — most sites stop elevated work at 25-30 mph

confined_space_entry.definition: Space large enough to enter, limited entry/exit, not designed for continuous occupancy (vessels, tanks, columns, exchangers, large pipe >24 inch)
PPE: 4-gas monitor (O2, LEL, CO, H2S) — CONTINUOUS monitoring
SCBA or airline respirator if atmosphere IDLH or unknown
Full body harness with retrieval line
Non-sparking tools if flammable atmosphere possible

atmospheric_limits.LEL: Less than 10% of LEL for entry
atmospheric_limits.H2S: 10 ppm 8-hr TWA, 20 ppm ceiling, 50 ppm IDLH

permits_required: Confined space entry permit (valid one shift only)
Hot work permit if welding inside
Blind/blank list verified (all connecting piping isolated)
JSA_items: Atmosphere tested BEFORE entry — top, middle, bottom of space
Continuous atmospheric monitoring during work
Attendant at entry point at ALL times — never leaves
Communication between entrant and attendant verified
Rescue plan in place — on-site rescue team or fire department notified
Retrieval system rigged (tripod and winch for vertical entry)
All connecting piping blinded or double-blocked-and-bled
Electrical isolation of any equipment in the space
Ventilation — continuous forced air, fresh air inlet at bottom, exhaust at top
Rescue harness worn with retrieval line attached
Hot work inside confined space: 3 separate permits needed (confined space + hot work + safe work)
No compressed gas bottles inside confined space
Welding machine and gas bottles OUTSIDE, leads/hoses routed in

hydrostatic_testing.purpose: Pressure test completed piping systems with water to verify integrity
hydrostatic_testing.test_pressure: 1.5x design pressure (ASME B31.3) held for minimum 10 minutes, typically 4 hours in refinery work
PPE: Face shield when checking connections under pressure
hazards: Catastrophic failure — high energy water release
Joint/flange leaks — high pressure water jet can cut skin
Trapped air — acts like pneumatic test, much more dangerous
Test equipment failure (gauges, hoses, pumps)
Dropped test blinds (heavy)
Slip hazards from water
Cold weather — freeze damage to piping and gauges
Water hammer from rapid valve operation
JSA_items: Verify test package complete — P&IDs marked, test boundaries identified
ALL temporary blinds rated for test pressure
Vent ALL high points to remove trapped air — CRITICAL
Fill from lowest point, vent from highest
Pressure up slowly — 25% increments with hold and inspection
Barricade test zone — no unauthorized personnel
Test gauges calibrated and within range (25-75% of gauge range)
Relief device set at 110% of test pressure or test pump has pressure limiting
Visual inspection at 50% of test pressure
Hold at test pressure and walk down ALL joints — look for leaks, distortion
Document: start time, pressure, temperature, hold time, sign-offs
Drain plan — where does test water go? Environmental requirements
If glycol used (freeze protection), contain and dispose properly

pneumatic_testing.purpose: Pressure test with air or nitrogen when water cannot be used (catalyst systems, systems that cannot be dried, air-sensitive materials)
pneumatic_testing.WARNING: PNEUMATIC TESTS ARE EXTREMELY DANGEROUS — stored energy in compressed gas is exponentially higher than hydro. A pneumatic test failure is equivalent to an EXPLOSION.
PPE: Full PPE — hard hat, safety glasses, steel-toe boots
Face shield when approaching test zone
hazards: EXPLOSIVE FAILURE — pneumatic tests store enormous energy
Shrapnel from pipe or fitting failure
Blast wave — can cause injury even without contact
Missile hazard from blown blinds or gaskets
Noise from leak or failure can cause permanent hearing damage
Asphyxiation risk if testing with nitrogen in enclosed area
permits_required: Pneumatic test permit (higher level approval than hydro, often requires engineering manager sign-off)
Barricade/exclusion zone — LARGER than hydro test zone
JSA_items: Engineering approval REQUIRED for all pneumatic tests
Pressure up in stages: 25 psi initial hold and check, then 50% of test pressure, then increments of 10% to test pressure
EVERYONE out of test zone during pressure-up above 25 psi
Extended exclusion zone — typically 100 ft or more per site policy
Hold at test pressure for 10 minutes minimum
Walk-down for leaks only AFTER full pressure hold is stable
Use soapy water for leak detection, NEVER use hands to feel for leaks
Nitrogen purge required if system had hydrocarbons
Oxygen monitoring if nitrogen test — N2 displaces O2
Pressure relief device MANDATORY
Document everything — multiple witness signatures required

hot_tapping.definition: Cutting into a live (pressurized, in-service) pipe or vessel to create a new connection without shutdown
PPE: Welding PPE if welding the fitting
hazards: Welding on pressurized, possibly hydrocarbon-containing pipe
Product release if burn-through occurs
Fire/explosion if flammable service
H2S exposure in sour service
Hot product release during coupon removal
Pipe wall too thin for welding (minimum wall thickness check CRITICAL)
JSA_items: UT scan pipe wall — minimum remaining wall must support weld without burn-through
PMI verify pipe material — must match welding procedure
Process conditions reviewed — temperature, pressure, product, flow
Minimum wall thickness typically 0.25 inch for hot tap welding
Valve on fitting tested before cutting
Qualified hot tap crew and machine operator
Fire suppression standing by
Area gas tested continuously
Downstream effects of new connection evaluated

line_breaking.definition: Opening a sealed piping system — breaking flanges, removing valves, cutting pipe in a system that has been in service
PPE: Chemical splash goggles or face shield
SCBA or airline respirator available if H2S/toxic service
hazards: Residual product release — even 'drained' lines contain product in low spots
H2S release from sour service (deadly at 100 ppm)
Chemical burns (caustic, acid services)
Benzene exposure (carcinogen — 1 ppm TWA limit)
LEL/fire risk from residual hydrocarbons
Trapped pressure behind valves
Product in dead legs and low points
permits_required: Line break permit (also called safe work permit)
JSA_items: Verify line has been drained, depressured, and purged per procedure
Isolation verified — double block and bleed or blind
Loosen bolts on far side first (away from you), gradually break seal
Have containment ready (drip tray, absorbent, drain connection)
Monitor atmosphere continuously — LEL, H2S, benzene
Know the product and have SDS available
Emergency escape route identified (upwind)
Wind direction evaluated — position upwind
Decon/wash station nearby for chemical services

steam_blowing.definition: Using high-pressure steam to clean new piping systems of construction debris (weld slag, scale, debris) before commissioning
PPE: Full body coverage — steam burns are severe
hazards: EXTREME noise (can cause permanent hearing damage)
Steam burns — invisible superheated steam is most dangerous
Thermal expansion of pipe — can move pipe off supports
Missile hazard from debris blown out of pipe
Trip hazards from temporary piping and silencer
Burns from touching hot pipe (600F+ surface temperature)
JSA_items: Exclusion zone around blow path — 200 ft minimum typical
Verify all temporary supports and restraints in place
Target plate installed at blow point to verify cleanliness
All spring hangers locked/pinned during steam blow
Verify expansion loops and guides can handle thermal movement
Blowdown path clear of personnel, equipment, and structures
Silencer (if used) properly installed and anchored
Communication plan — radio contact between control room and field
Monitor pipe movement during each blow — stop if excessive
Multiple blows required — polish target plate between blows until clean

chemical_cleaning.definition: Circulating chemical solutions through piping to remove mill scale, rust, oil, and construction debris
common_chemicals: Citric acid (mild, common for stainless systems)
Hydrochloric acid (inhibited) for carbon steel descaling
PPE: Chemical splash goggles and face shield
Chemical-resistant suit (Tyvek minimum, PVC for acid)
SCBA available for acid/toxic fume potential
hazards: Chemical burns — acid and alkaline solutions
Toxic fume generation (especially HCl)
Hydrogen generation during acid cleaning (explosion risk)
Environmental spill
Pump and hose failure — high pressure chemical release
Mixing incompatible chemicals (acid + bleach = chlorine gas)
JSA_items: SDS for ALL chemicals reviewed with crew
Containment for all chemical solutions — no ground release
Neutralization chemicals on-site and ready
Emergency shower and eyewash tested
Spill kit appropriate for chemicals in use
Continuous atmospheric monitoring
Hydrogen monitor if acid cleaning (H2 generation)
Chemical waste disposal plan in place
Temporary piping rated for chemical service and pressure
All connections checked for leaks at low pressure before increasing

universal_safety_reminders.stop_work_authority: EVERY worker has the RIGHT and OBLIGATION to stop work they believe is unsafe. No retaliation.
universal_safety_reminders.JSA_requirement: Job Safety Analysis must be completed and reviewed with ALL crew members before starting ANY task. Update JSA when conditions change.
universal_safety_reminders.emergency_numbers: Know your site emergency number, muster point, nearest first aid, AED, and emergency shower/eyewash locations
universal_safety_reminders.reporting: Report ALL incidents, near-misses, and unsafe conditions immediately. Near-misses are FREE LESSONS.
universal_safety_reminders.housekeeping: Clean as you go. Organized work areas prevent injuries. Secure all materials and tools against falling.
universal_safety_reminders.heat_stress: Monitor crew for heat illness — confusion, heavy sweating that stops, rapid pulse. Cool and hydrate. Call 911 for heat stroke.
universal_safety_reminders.cold_stress: Monitor for hypothermia and frostbite. Provide warming shelters and warm fluids. Watch for shivering that stops (dangerous sign).
